company.name: Mattermost
company.description: Self-hosted and cloud collaboration platform for national security and critical infrastructure enterprises, in their words the leading sovereign collaboration and AI automation platform. Ships an agentic AI product (Mattermost Agents) into regulated and classified-adjacent environments, holds SOC 2 Type II and ISO 27001:2022, operates as a DIB contractor under DFARS obligations, and reaches federal buyers today through a partner's FedRAMP Class D package rather than its own listing.

rootFiles.note: Drift monitor, evidence validator, report generator.

flow.label: company.yaml
flow.title: One config file
flow.body: Frameworks, data types, AI posture, listings, stack. Edit this one file and re-run the scaffold — the whole program re-renders. Adding a framework is a crosswalk mapping, never a new control.

flow.label: GitHub Action
flow.title: The scaffold renders
flow.body: An Action filters the control library to the company's frameworks and turns on the pillars and families the config requires.

flow.label: Daily checks
flow.title: Control health is computed
flow.body: Scheduled jobs read the systems of record and compute pass or fail for each control. Code decides; the system of record supplies the evidence.

flow.label: healthy / drift
flow.title: Green, or a drift Issue
flow.body: Healthy → status stays green and evidence is recorded. Drift → a GitHub Issue opens automatically with the control ID, owner, framework impact, and evidence needed.
flow.tag: the Issue IS the due diligence

flow.label: AI drafts
flow.title: AI drafts the fix and the narrative
flow.body: AI proposes the remediation and writes the auditor-facing narrative and gap analysis. It never authors evidence; evidence is computed from systems of record and rejected by schema if model-generated.

flow.label: Pull Request
flow.title: A human gate: the Pull Request
flow.body: A named function reviews the AI-drafted remediation. The merge is the authorization. Nothing becomes record without a human approving it first.

flow.label: git history
flow.title: Git history is the audit trail
flow.body: On merge, the control status updates and the commit records who decided what, when, and why. The trail is immutable and already exists — audit prep is a query, not a reconstruction.

frictionPoints.title: The first success metric predates the CMMC suspension
frictionPoints.body: The req's first success metric (CMMC Level 2 gap assessment and readiness roadmap in 90 days) was published before the DoW CIO suspended CMMC Phase II. Third-party assessments cannot currently be required, while NIST 800-171 Rev 2 and DFARS 7012 remain fully enforced through self-assessment. The work is still right and its destination moved, so the plan here forks cleanly on the Reform Task Force report (~11 September) rather than executing an instruction written for the prior regime.

frictionPoints.title: Self-assessment raised the evidence bar, and most programs will read it as relief
frictionPoints.body: With no C3PAO standing between the company and the government, a posted SPRS score is a signed federal claim with False Claims Act exposure and an active DOJ enforcement initiative behind it. The evidence standard becomes: nothing affirmed that cannot be re-derived from a system of record on demand. That is an argument for computed, continuously monitored evidence, which is exactly what this engine does.

frictionPoints.title: Handbook-as-code, but the governed artifacts live in Drata
frictionPoints.body: The public handbook is genuinely good docs-as-code: git source, PR workflow, machine-readable index. The policies themselves live in Drata behind a login, and the governance content lacks the merge gates and ownership rules the product side already enforces (86 CI workflows on the monorepo, zero on the handbook). The plumbing for policy-as-code exists and is culturally normal here; the work is extending proven enforcement patterns to the governance surface, with Drata's role decided deliberately.

frictionPoints.title: Public compliance claims disagree with each other
frictionPoints.body: One public page says an ATO is being acquired while another says one is held via Platform One's continuous ATO; FedRAMP status is described in vocabulary the program retired in May 2026 (High/Authorized became Class D/Certified). Normal drift for a fast-moving program without a single claims owner, and a one-week claims-consistency pass with sourced corrections fixes it. Precision on inherited-versus-held is the distinction federal buyers actually test.

frictionPoints.title: An AI company whose internal AI policy is not publicly visible
frictionPoints.body: Mattermost ships agentic AI to defense buyers and publishes AI risk guidance for customers' CISOs, and no internal AI usage policy is publicly findable (if one exists, it is in Drata). The sharpest version: staff LLM use touching export-controlled data is an EAR/ITAR question, and defense customers will ask through the questionnaire queue this role owns. Their own Agents V2 approval model already contains the right vocabulary; the internal policy can inherit an argument their engineers already won.

frictionPoints.title: The questionnaire answer bank asserts; it does not evidence
frictionPoints.body: The public security answer bank is a long run of bare yes answers with no links to controls or artifacts, and at least one probable copy-paste defect is visible from outside, surviving precisely because unreviewed yes columns never get read closely. Wiring each answer to a control and its evidence, behind a lightweight review gate, turns the deal-cycle metric from assertion into something checkable.

controls.title: AI and autonomous technologies governance
controls.owner: GRC + Security (Legal/Privacy joint on lawful basis)
controls.why: Mattermost operates an agentic layer over its channel collaboration graph. Autonomous systems that hold privileged access need a control for identity, least privilege, decision logging, and a human gate on high-impact actions — the hero control.
controls.statement: Policies for AI and autonomous-technology risk are defined, owned, and enforced. Autonomous agents read personal data only under purpose-bound authorization, at minimum scope, with full decision logging, and with human accountability for high-impact actions. No agent holds standing access to sensitive production data.

controls.title: Periodic review of account privileges
controls.why: Periodic access review is the access pillar of identity and operations governance, the control auditors test first. It limits standing access to personal data.
controls.statement: Privileges for people and service accounts are reviewed on a fixed cadence against role and employment, and unjustified access is removed. Leaver deprovisioning fires from the human-resources system of record, not a manual ticket. Service and other non-human accounts are held to the same bar as human accounts.

controls.title: Configuration change control
controls.why: Change control is a pillar of identity and operations governance. In a source-control-native program the pull request is the gate, and the evidence is a byproduct of shipping rather than reconstructed for an audit.
controls.statement: Technical configuration change reaches production only through a peer-reviewed pull request linked to a tracked work item, with required checks passing. Direct pushes to protected branches are blocked, the reviewer is independent of the author, and emergency changes follow a documented after-the-fact review.

controls.title: Continuous monitoring and event logging
controls.why: Continuous monitoring turns a point-in-time audit into a standing program. It is the engine of the drift-opens-an-Issue loop that makes due diligence visible.
controls.statement: Enterprise monitoring computes control health continuously. Security events ship to a central append-only store with retention and integrity protection. Logs establish what, when, where, source, outcome, and identity, and defined event types alert a monitored queue with an acknowledgement SLA.

controls.title: Third-party management
controls.why: Sensitive data flows to subprocessors and model providers. Diligence depth must match what a party can touch across channel collaboration graph, and trust evidence a vendor already produced should be reused, not re-collected.
controls.statement: Third parties with system or personal-data access are tiered by what they can touch, assessed before onboarding, and bound by security, breach-notice, and processing terms. High-risk parties reassess on cadence, and vendor trust evidence is reused rather than re-questionnaired.

controls.title: Incident response operations
controls.why: When an agent exceeds its scope or a breach clock starts, the response has to be rehearsed and the timeline defensible — including incidents that involve autonomous-agent scope on personal data.
controls.statement: Incidents are detected, triaged, contained, and recorded against a governed plan, including AI scope breaches. Legal/Privacy owns notification within statutory timelines, and every incident receives a post-incident review.

controls.title: Use of cryptographic controls
controls.why: Use of cryptographic controls — defined once and rendered into every in-scope framework's view.
controls.statement: Sensitive and regulated data is protected with cryptographic controls that meet a defined standard, in transit and at rest. Keys are generated, stored, rotated, and revoked under a documented key-management process, separated from the data they protect. Weak or deprecated algorithms and protocols are not used, and exceptions are tracked and time-bound.

controls.title: Vulnerability and patch management
controls.why: Vulnerability and patch management — defined once and rendered into every in-scope framework's view.
controls.statement: Technical vulnerabilities across code, dependencies, images, and infrastructure are discovered continuously, triaged by severity and exposure, and remediated within defined service levels. Internet-facing and sensitive-data systems carry the tightest timelines. Unremediated vulnerabilities past SLA are tracked as exceptions with a named owner and a closure plan.

controls.title: Data classification and protection
controls.why: Data classification and protection — defined once and rendered into every in-scope framework's view.
controls.statement: Data is classified by sensitivity, and personal data are designated the highest-sensitivity class. Handling, retention, and disposal rules attach to the classification and are enforced. Data is minimized to what the declared purpose requires and deleted or restricted when the lawful basis ends. Cleartext flows of the highest-sensitivity classes are inventoried so every other control knows its scope.

controls.title: Security awareness and role-based training
controls.why: Mattermost operates an agentic layer over its channel collaboration graph. Autonomous systems that hold privileged access need a control for identity, least privilege, decision logging, and a human gate on high-impact actions — the hero control.
controls.statement: Personnel complete security and privacy awareness training on hire and on a recurring cadence, and role-based training is delivered to functions with elevated access or specific obligations. Training covers the risks specific to this service: precise-location and minor-data handling, social-engineering resistance, and the governance of the agentic AI layer. Completion is tracked, and non-completion for in-scope roles is an exception with a named owner.

controls.title: Security and privacy governance program
controls.why: Security and privacy governance program — defined once and rendered into every in-scope framework's view.
controls.statement: A documented security and privacy program defines scope, ownership, and objectives, is approved by leadership, and is reviewed at least annually. Authority and accountability for the program are assigned to a named function.

controls.title: Policy hierarchy and periodic review
controls.why: Policy hierarchy and periodic review — defined once and rendered into every in-scope framework's view.
controls.statement: Policies, standards, and procedures form a maintained hierarchy with named owners, a review cadence, and version control. Each document is reviewed and re-approved on cadence or on material change.

controls.title: Roles, responsibilities, and segregation of duties
controls.why: Roles, responsibilities, and segregation of duties — defined once and rendered into every in-scope framework's view.
controls.statement: Security responsibilities are assigned by role and documented in a RACI. Conflicting duties are separated; where separation is impractical, a compensating control is recorded.

controls.title: Program metrics and leadership reporting
controls.why: Program metrics and leadership reporting — defined once and rendered into every in-scope framework's view.
controls.statement: Control health and program performance are measured and reported to leadership on a fixed cadence, with material risks escalated. Metrics are computed from systems of record, not assembled by hand.

controls.title: Risk assessment methodology
controls.why: Risk assessment methodology — defined once and rendered into every in-scope framework's view.
controls.statement: Risk is assessed using a documented, repeatable method that estimates likelihood and impact in defined terms. Assessments run on cadence and on significant change.

controls.title: Risk register and treatment
controls.why: Risk register and treatment — defined once and rendered into every in-scope framework's view.
controls.statement: Identified risks are recorded in a register with an owner, treatment decision, and target date. Accepted risk is approved at an authority level matched to its severity.

controls.title: Asset inventory and ownership
controls.why: Asset inventory and ownership — defined once and rendered into every in-scope framework's view.
controls.statement: A maintained inventory records hardware, software, services, and data stores with an owner for each. The inventory is reconciled from authoritative discovery, not a manual list.

controls.title: Data and asset classification
controls.why: Data and asset classification — defined once and rendered into every in-scope framework's view.
controls.statement: Assets and data are classified by sensitivity against a defined scheme, and handling requirements follow from the classification.

controls.title: Secure disposal of media and equipment
controls.why: Secure disposal of media and equipment — defined once and rendered into every in-scope framework's view.
controls.statement: Media and equipment holding confidential data are sanitized or destroyed before disposal or reuse, with the action recorded.

controls.title: Access provisioning from joiner-mover-leaver
controls.why: Periodic access review is the access pillar of identity and operations governance, the control auditors test first. It limits standing access to personal data.
controls.statement: Access is granted, changed, and revoked from authoritative HR events. Leaver access is removed on the same day the leaver event fires; the feed is the trigger, not a ticket.

controls.title: Least privilege and privileged access management
controls.why: Periodic access review is the access pillar of identity and operations governance, the control auditors test first. It limits standing access to personal data.
controls.statement: Access follows least privilege. Privileged access is brokered, time-bound where feasible, and separately logged. Standing administrative access is minimized.

controls.title: Authentication and multi-factor enforcement
controls.why: Periodic access review is the access pillar of identity and operations governance, the control auditors test first. It limits standing access to personal data.
controls.statement: Users authenticate with unique identities and phishing-resistant multi-factor where supported. MFA is enforced for remote and administrative access.

controls.title: Service and non-human identity governance
controls.why: Periodic access review is the access pillar of identity and operations governance, the control auditors test first. It limits standing access to personal data.
controls.statement: Service accounts, API keys, and workload identities are registered with an owner, scoped to least privilege, and rotated or expired. No standing secret without an owner.

controls.title: Baseline configuration and hardening
controls.why: Change control is a pillar of identity and operations governance. In a source-control-native program the pull request is the gate, and the evidence is a byproduct of shipping rather than reconstructed for an audit.
controls.statement: Systems are built from approved, hardened baselines, and deviations are detected and reconciled. Baselines follow a recognized hardening standard.

controls.title: Key management
controls.why: Key management — defined once and rendered into every in-scope framework's view.
controls.statement: Cryptographic keys are generated, stored, rotated, and retired under a defined lifecycle, with access to key material restricted and logged.

controls.title: Data retention and disposal
controls.why: Regulated personal data is processed only on a lawful basis, with the data subject's rights honored end to end across channel collaboration graph.
controls.statement: Data is retained only as long as required by a defined schedule and is disposed of when the schedule expires, with disposal recorded.

controls.title: Privacy notice and lawful basis
controls.why: Regulated personal data is processed only on a lawful basis, with the data subject's rights honored end to end across channel collaboration graph.
controls.statement: Personal data is processed for a stated purpose under a documented lawful basis, and a plain-language notice describes what is collected, why, and the individual's rights.

controls.title: Data subject rights handling
controls.why: Regulated personal data is processed only on a lawful basis, with the data subject's rights honored end to end across channel collaboration graph.
controls.statement: Requests to access, correct, delete, or port personal data are honored within statutory timelines, with identity verified before action.

controls.title: Log integrity and retention
controls.why: Continuous monitoring turns a point-in-time audit into a standing program. It is the engine of the drift-opens-an-Issue loop that makes due diligence visible.
controls.statement: Logs are protected against alteration with write-once or equivalent integrity controls and retained for a defined period. Clocks are synchronized to an authoritative source.

controls.title: Network segmentation and boundary protection
controls.why: Network segmentation and boundary protection — defined once and rendered into every in-scope framework's view.
controls.statement: Networks are segmented by trust and sensitivity, and traffic crossing boundaries is filtered by default-deny rules that are reviewed on cadence.

controls.title: Secure remote access
controls.why: Secure remote access — defined once and rendered into every in-scope framework's view.
controls.statement: Remote access to internal resources is authenticated, encrypted, and least-privilege, and administrative access originates from controlled paths.

controls.title: Secure development gates
controls.why: Secure development gates — defined once and rendered into every in-scope framework's view.
controls.statement: Security requirements and checks are built into design, build, test, and deploy stages, and a release cannot pass without its required gates emitting evidence.

controls.title: Software supply chain and dependency integrity
controls.why: Software supply chain and dependency integrity — defined once and rendered into every in-scope framework's view.
controls.statement: Third-party and open-source components are inventoried, scanned for known vulnerabilities, and verified for integrity before use. Build provenance is recorded.

controls.title: Secrets management
controls.why: Secrets management — defined once and rendered into every in-scope framework's view.
controls.statement: Secrets are stored in a managed vault, never in source or configuration, and are scanned for in code. Exposed secrets are rotated on detection.

controls.title: Penetration testing
controls.why: Penetration testing — defined once and rendered into every in-scope framework's view.
controls.statement: Independent testing exercises the environment on cadence and after significant change, and findings feed the remediation queue and the risk register.

controls.title: Endpoint protection and detection
controls.why: Endpoint protection and detection — defined once and rendered into every in-scope framework's view.
controls.statement: Endpoints run managed protection that detects and responds to malicious activity, with coverage and health monitored centrally.

controls.title: Device management and hardening
controls.why: Device management and hardening — defined once and rendered into every in-scope framework's view.
controls.statement: Managed devices enforce configuration, disk encryption, and screen lock, and access from unmanaged devices to confidential data is restricted.

controls.title: Breach notification
controls.why: When an agent exceeds its scope or a breach clock starts, the response has to be rehearsed and the timeline defensible — including incidents that involve autonomous-agent scope on personal data.
controls.statement: When an incident meets a statutory or contractual threshold, affected parties and regulators are notified within required windows, with the decision and its basis recorded.

controls.title: Business continuity and disaster recovery
controls.why: Business continuity and disaster recovery — defined once and rendered into every in-scope framework's view.
controls.statement: Recovery objectives are set from a business impact analysis, and continuity and recovery plans are documented, resourced, and tested on cadence.

controls.title: Backup and restoration
controls.why: Backup and restoration — defined once and rendered into every in-scope framework's view.
controls.statement: Critical data and systems are backed up on a defined schedule with backups protected and restoration verified by test, not assumption.

controls.title: Subprocessor and data-flow assurance
controls.why: Sensitive data flows to subprocessors and model providers. Diligence depth must match what a party can touch across channel collaboration graph, and trust evidence a vendor already produced should be reused, not re-collected.
controls.statement: Where personal or sensitive data flows to subprocessors, processing terms, breach-notice windows, and transfer mechanisms are in place and recorded.

controls.title: Personnel screening and agreements
controls.why: Personnel screening and agreements — defined once and rendered into every in-scope framework's view.
controls.statement: Personnel are screened proportionate to role before access, and acknowledge security and confidentiality obligations. A disciplinary path exists for violations.

controls.title: Physical access and environmental protection
controls.why: Physical access and environmental protection — defined once and rendered into every in-scope framework's view.
controls.statement: Physical access to facilities and data-bearing equipment is restricted, logged, and reviewed. Where infrastructure is cloud-hosted, provider attestations are inherited and tracked.

controls.title: Model and prompt change management
controls.why: Mattermost operates an agentic layer over its channel collaboration graph. Autonomous systems that hold privileged access need a control for identity, least privilege, decision logging, and a human gate on high-impact actions — the hero control.
controls.statement: Changes to models, prompts, and tool grants follow change control with pre-deployment evaluation, drift checks, rollback, and an approval gate.

milestones: Current-state map of every owned program: Drata contents, audit calendar, SSP/POA&M/SPRS state, risk register, TPRM, questionnaire flow.
Assessment boundary agreed: which entity, which systems, CUI determination; recalibrated day-90 deliverable in writing.
Regulatory response memo absorbing the ~11 Sep Task Force report the day it lands, with the FedRAMP Class A / 20x decision framed for leadership.
Visible fixes routed with owners: claims-consistency pass, answer-bank review, published audit calendar.
roadmap.aiAccelerates: AI drafts the current-state narratives and the regulatory memo from primary sources in days, with every claim linked; a human approves before anything circulates.

roadmap.theme: Execute the assessment, pilot computed evidence
milestones: CMMC L2 self-assessment across all 110 requirements of 800-171 Rev 2 on the agreed boundary, every met backed by re-derivable evidence.
SSP current, POA&M live with owners and dates, SPRS position reconciled; the affirmation workflow defined signature-grade.
Continuous-monitoring pilot live on one control family, reading Drata and source systems, drift opening tickets same-day.
Architecture decision memo on Drata's role, priced honestly; AI usage policy drafted in their own Agents V2 vocabulary.
roadmap.aiAccelerates: AI drafts implementation statements, POA&M entries, and the decision memo; evidence stays deterministically computed and is schema-rejected if model-generated.

roadmap.theme: Deliver metric one, institutionalize the rest
milestones: Gap assessment and regime-aware readiness roadmap delivered, built to survive whichever branch the Task Force outcome takes.
Monitoring extended to the families the audit calendar needs next, with a published count of manual evidence retired.
Answer bank evidence-linked and review-gated; trust center owned and current; turnaround measured.
Team growth proposal from 90 days of volume data; 90-day report where every claim links to its artifact.
roadmap.aiAccelerates: AI drafts the roadmap narratives, gap workpapers, and trust collateral in days rather than weeks, with a named human approving everything that becomes record.

collateral.note: The full phase-by-phase plan behind the roadmap above, mapped to the role's five published success metrics.

collateral.name: hiring-manager-questions.md
collateral.note: The questions whose answers change the plan, ranked, with what each decides.

collateral.name: runbooks/
collateral.note: Executable procedures: the L2 self-assessment under the suspension, the monitoring pilot, the customer-assurance pass, the AI policy.

collateral.name: context/
collateral.note: The req decoded, the regulatory clock, their GitHub org mapped, the full framework landscape, and the engine bridge.

guardrails: Built from public research only. No claim is made about Mattermost's internal security posture.
AI drafts narratives and remediations; a human approves before anything becomes record. Evidence is computed from systems of record, never authored by a model.
Functions and roles are named — never individuals. Accountability attaches to a function.

stack.name: aws
stack.role: cloud — evidence source for access, change, ops

stack.name: github
stack.role: code — the operating model lives here; change evidence is the merge

stack.name: gitbook
stack.role: docs — stakeholder reports render here after review

stack.name: mattermost
stack.role: comms — drift, TPRM, and deadline notifications route here

stack.name: drata
stack.role: GRC tool — the audit-facing interface; this repo stays the system of record
